text: The surgeon performs a right hemicolectomy on an elderly patient, using a four-port approach. An extra port is visible on the screen. The surgeon dissects around the iliocolic pedicle, thinning it out with a ligature device, while sweeping down the retroperitoneum to access the optimal surgical plane.

The surgeon continues to thin out the ileocolic pedicle using a ligature, ensuring no excess fat remains for a complete seal. The artery and vein are taken, and the duodenum is briefly checked, revealing a visible lymph node. The dissection proceeds medially to laterally, sweeping the duodenum off the right colon mesentery. The right colic artery comes into view, along with the pancreas, as the surgeon thins out the right colic pedicle.

The surgeon takes the right colic artery and vein, then proceeds to dissect straight up to the colon margin, removing the remaining mesentery up to the planned distal margin. The medial dissection is completed, extending laterally to facilitate the subsequent lateral dissection, with the surgeon reaching beyond the colon wall to the planned margin.

The surgeon begins the lateral dissection over the transverse colon, splitting the omentum to the planned transection level. The dissection progresses to gain entry into the lesser sac, the space between the colon and the liver, with the previously dissected medial space starting to come into view.

The surgeon finishes detaching the hepatic flexure, revealing the medial dissected space where the mesentery was transected. The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure and approaches the tumor, which appears firm and fixed to the abdominal wall, indicating a fibrotic response. Despite the tumor's appearance, the margins are clean.

The surgeon approaches the tumor, which is obstructing the appendix, causing it to appear enlarged and dilated. The surgeon adjusts the angle using a right-handed instrument to better access the tumor, which is located high on the abdominal sidewall. Without ureteral stents, the surgeon manipulates the tumor and uses a ligature to dissect the small bowel mesentery up to the ileal margin for improved access.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the tumor off the abdominal sidewall, having previously checked for the ureter in the area. The mesentery has been taken to create a window for easier dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection by addressing the remaining lateral attachments of the colon. The procedure progresses towards the final stages of the colectomy.
